title: Defining a class with typed properties
instruction:
A TypeScript class declares its properties and their types at the top of the class body. TypeScript checks that the constructor initializes them correctly.

Create a `BankAccount` class.
successMessage: TypeScript checks that `owner`, `balance`, and `currency` are initialized in the constructor and match their declared types. Missing a property declaration causes a compile error.

title: Constructor shorthand — declare and assign in one step
instruction:
Prefixing constructor parameters with `public`, `private`, or `readonly` automatically declares them as class properties. This eliminates the boilerplate of separate declarations and assignments.

Refactor the BankAccount to use constructor shorthand.
successMessage: Constructor shorthand is equivalent to declaring the property and assigning it in the body. `private balance` is only accessible inside the class — external code must use `getBalance()`. This enforces encapsulation.

title: Access modifiers — public, private, protected
instruction:
Access modifiers control who can access a class member. `public` (default) — anyone. `private` — only the class itself. `protected` — the class and its subclasses.

Explore access modifiers with an employee class hierarchy.
expectedOutput:
Alice (Engineering) — ID: 1 — manages: Bob, Carol, Dan
  (ID accessible: 1)
successMessage: `protected employeeId` is accessible in `Manager` (a subclass) but not from outside the class hierarchy. `private salary` is only accessible within `Employee` itself — `Manager` cannot read it directly.

title: Getters and setters — controlled property access
instruction:
Getters (`get`) and setters (`set`) let you run logic when a property is read or written. They look like properties to callers but behave like methods.

Add validation to a temperature class using getters/setters.
successMessage: Callers use `temp.celsius = 37` (looks like property assignment) but the setter validates the input. Computed getters (`fahrenheit`, `kelvin`) recalculate every time they're accessed.

title: Static members — class-level state and methods
instruction:
Static members belong to the class itself, not instances. They're used for utility methods, factories, and shared state across all instances.

Build an ID counter and factory methods using static members.
successMessage: `static nextId` is shared across all instances — each new User gets a unique, auto-incrementing ID. `static createAdmin` is a factory method — an alternative constructor with a specific signature.

title: Inheritance — extending classes
instruction:
TypeScript classes can extend other classes. The subclass inherits all public and protected members and can override methods. `super()` calls the parent constructor.

Build a vehicle hierarchy.
successMessage: `override` keyword (TypeScript 4.3+) explicitly marks that you're overriding a parent method. If the parent doesn't have that method, TypeScript errors — preventing accidental method name typos.

title: Abstract classes — enforce subclass contracts
instruction:
Abstract classes define the structure that subclasses must implement. You can't instantiate an abstract class directly — it's a template.

Build a report generator framework using abstract classes.
successMessage: Abstract classes define the skeleton of an algorithm. `render()` is inherited and calls `generateContent()` and `getFormat()` — methods that subclasses must provide. If a subclass doesn't implement them, TypeScript errors.

title: Implementing interfaces — explicit contracts
instruction:
A class can implement one or more interfaces. This explicitly declares that the class fulfills a contract — TypeScript then verifies it does.

Build a storage system with swappable backends.
successMessage: `implements Storage<T>` tells TypeScript to verify that `MemoryStorage` provides all methods defined in the interface. Missing any method is a compile error. You could swap in a `RedisStorage` or `LocalStorage` implementation without changing call sites.

title: Private fields (#) — truly private at runtime
instruction:
TypeScript's `private` keyword is compile-time only. JavaScript's `#` fields are truly private at runtime — they cannot be accessed or enumerated outside the class, even with `as any` or reflection.

Compare TypeScript `private` with JavaScript `#` fields.
successMessage: `#secretKey` is encapsulated at the JavaScript runtime level — no bypass is possible. `private label` is only enforced by TypeScript — at runtime it's a regular property. Use `#` for security-sensitive data.

title: Mixins — composing class behavior
instruction:
Mixins add reusable behaviors to classes without deep inheritance hierarchies. TypeScript supports mixin patterns through function-based composition.

Compose auditing and serialization into a class.
expectedOutput: JSON keys: createdAt, updatedAt, name, email
successMessage: Mixins compose behaviors without inheritance chains. `TimestampedSerializableUser` has `createdAt`, `updatedAt`, `touch()`, and `toJSON()` — all from the two mixin functions. The date will match today's date when you run this.

title: Class decorators — metadata and transformation
instruction:
Decorators (experimental, widely used in frameworks like Angular and NestJS) add metadata or transform a class. TypeScript supports them with `experimentalDecorators`.

Simulate a basic decorator pattern manually.
successMessage: This demonstrates what decorators do under the hood — they replace a method with a wrapper that adds behavior before/after the call. In NestJS and Angular, `@Injectable()`, `@Get()`, and similar decorators work exactly like this.

title: Type-safe event emitter class
instruction:
A common pattern is building a type-safe event emitter where TypeScript knows exactly what payload each event carries. This prevents subscribing to non-existent events or receiving the wrong payload type.

Build a typed event emitter.
successMessage: TypeScript knows the payload type for each event — `emitter.on("login", e => e.userId)` gives `e.userId` as `number`. Emitting `emitter.emit("login", { code: 429 })` would be a type error. This is how production event systems are built.

title: Class with generic type parameter
instruction:
Classes can have generic type parameters, just like functions and interfaces. This lets you build reusable data structures that work with any type.

Build a generic Stack data structure.
successMessage: `Stack<T>` works for any type — `Stack<number>`, `Stack<string>`, `Stack<User>`. TypeScript ensures you can only push values of the correct type and that `pop()` returns the correct type.
